FINAL REPORT
 REASON FOR EXAMINATION:  Evaluation of the patient with upper gastrointestinal
 bleeding after intubation. Assessment for ET tube placement.
 
 Portable AP radiograph of the chest was reviewed.
 
 The ET tube tip is 4.3 cm above the carina.  The right internal jugular line
 tip is at the level of superior SVC.  Heart size and mediastinum are grossly
 unremarkable.  Left pleural effusion and left basal consolidation is
 demonstrated with effusion being most likely loculated.  The right lung is
 demonstrating right upper lung opacity, with broad differential diagnosis
 starting with infection, with potentially neoplasm present.  Correlation with
 outside cross-sectional imaging is recommended if available.  Otherwise, close
 followup with subsequent chest radiographs is required.
 
 Cystic lesion within the right proximal humerus is noted, partially imaged and
 may be correlated with dedicated views.

 unknown chronicity in the two vertebral bodies superior to the prior
 kyphoplasty.

 pulmonary pressures, or both.

 PORTABLE CHEST X-RAY, ___.
 
 COMPARISON:  Chest x-ray ___.
 
 FINDINGS:  Since the prior radiograph, the patient has been extubated and the
 nasogastric tube has been removed.  Lung volumes are increased with relative
 flattening of the hemidiaphragms, likely due to chronic obstructive pulmonary
 disease as recent CT neck documented the presence of emphysema at the lung
 apices.  Cardiomediastinal contours are within normal limits allowing for
 rotation apparently related to scoliosis.  Patchy and linear opacities are
 present at the right lung base, accompanied by volume loss with inferior
 displacement of the minor fissure.  The radiographic appearance favors
 atelectasis, but aspiration and infectious pneumonia could produce a similar
 appearance in the appropriate clinical setting.  Followup PA and lateral
 radiographs may be helpful in this regard when the patient's condition
 permits.  At that time, the costophrenic angles can be reassessed to
 determinate whether there are small bilateral pleural effusions.
